NKJV.1: Now after five days Ananias the high priest came down with the elders and a certain orator named Tertullus. These gave evidence to the governor against Paul.
NKJV.2: And when he was called upon, Tertullus began his accusation, saying: “Seeing that through you we enjoy great peace, and prosperity is being brought to this nation by your foresight,
NKJV.3: we accept it always and in all places, most noble Felix, with all thankfulness.
NKJV.4: Nevertheless, not to be tedious to you any further, I beg you to hear, by your courtesy, a few words from us.
NKJV.5: For we have found this man a plague, a creator of dissension among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes.
NKJV.6: He even tried to profane the temple, and we seized him, and wanted to judge him according to our law.
NKJV.7: But the commander Lysias came by and with great violence took him out of our hands,
NKJV.8: commanding his accusers to come to you. By examining him yourself you may ascertain all these things of which we accuse him.”
NKJV.9: And the Jews also assented, maintaining that these things were so.
NKJV.10: Then Paul, after the governor had nodded to him to speak, answered: “Inasmuch as I know that you have been for many years a judge of this nation, I do the more cheerfully answer for myself,
NKJV.11: because you may ascertain that it is no more than twelve days since I went up to Jerusalem to worship.
NKJV.12: And they neither found me in the temple disputing with anyone nor inciting the crowd, either in the synagogues or in the city.
NKJV.13: Nor can they prove the things of which they now accuse me.
NKJV.14: But this I confess to you, that according to the Way which they call a sect, so I worship the God of my fathers, believing all things which are written in the Law and in the Prophets.
NKJV.15: I have hope in God, which they themselves also accept, that there will be a resurrection of the dead, both of the just and the unjust.
NKJV.16: This being so, I myself always strive to have a conscience without offense toward God and men.
NKJV.17: “Now after many years I came to bring alms and offerings to my nation,
NKJV.18: in the midst of which some Jews from Asia found me purified in the temple, neither with a mob nor with tumult.
NKJV.19: They ought to have been here before you to object if they had anything against me.
NKJV.20: Or else let those who are here themselves say if they found any wrongdoing in me while I stood before the council,
NKJV.21: unless it is for this one statement which I cried out, standing among them, ‘Concerning the resurrection of the dead I am being judged by you this day.’ ”
NKJV.22: But when Felix heard these things, having more accurate knowledge of the Way, he adjourned the proceedings and said, “When Lysias the commander comes down, I will make a decision on your case.”
NKJV.23: So he commanded the centurion to keep Paul and to let him have liberty, and told him not to forbid any of his friends to provide for or visit him.
NKJV.24: And after some days, when Felix came with his wife Drusilla, who was Jewish, he sent for Paul and heard him concerning the faith in Christ.
NKJV.25: Now as he reasoned about righteousness, self-control, and the judgment to come, Felix was afraid and answered, “Go away for now; when I have a convenient time I will call for you.”
NKJV.26: Meanwhile he also hoped that money would be given him by Paul, that he might release him. Therefore he sent for him more often and conversed with him.
NKJV.27: But after two years Porcius Festus succeeded Felix; and Felix, wanting to do the Jews a favor, left Paul bound.

ESV.1: And after five days the high priest Ananias came down with some elders and a spokesman, one Tertullus. They laid before the governor their case against Paul.
ESV.2: And when he had been summoned, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying: “Since through you we enjoy much peace, and since by your foresight, most excellent Felix, reforms are being made for this nation,
ESV.3: in every way and everywhere we accept this with all gratitude.
ESV.4: But, to detain  you no further, I beg you in your kindness to hear us briefly.
ESV.5: For we have found this man a plague, one who stirs up riots among all the Jews throughout the world and is a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes.
ESV.6: He even tried to profane the temple, but we seized him.
ESV.8: By examining him yourself you will be able to find out from him about everything of which we accuse him.”
ESV.9: The Jews also joined in the charge, affirming that all these things were so.
ESV.10: And when the governor had nodded to him to speak, Paul replied: “Knowing that for many years you have been a judge over this nation, I cheerfully make my defense.
ESV.11: You can verify that it is not more than twelve days since I went up to worship in Jerusalem,
ESV.12: and they did not find me disputing with anyone or stirring up a crowd, either in the temple or in the synagogues or in the city.
ESV.13: Neither can they prove to you what they now bring up against me.
ESV.14: But this I confess to you, that according to the Way, which they call a sect, I worship the God of our fathers, believing everything laid down by the Law and written in the Prophets,
ESV.15: having a hope in God, which these men themselves accept, that there will be a resurrection of both the just and the unjust.
ESV.16: So I always take pains to have a clear conscience toward both God and man.
ESV.17: Now after several years I came to bring alms to my nation and to present offerings.
ESV.18: While I was doing this, they found me purified in the temple, without any crowd or tumult. But some Jews from Asia —
ESV.19: they ought to be here before you and to make an accusation, should they have anything against me.
ESV.20: Or else let these men themselves say what wrongdoing they found when I stood before the council,
ESV.21: other than this one thing that I cried out while standing among them: ‘It is with respect to the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial before you this day.’”
ESV.22: But Felix, having a rather accurate knowledge of the Way, put them off, saying, “When Lysias the tribune comes down, I will decide your case.”
ESV.23: Then he gave orders to the centurion that he should be kept in custody but have some liberty, and that none of his friends should be prevented from attending to his needs.
ESV.24: After some days Felix came with his wife Drusilla, who was Jewish, and he sent for Paul and heard him speak about faith in Christ Jesus.
ESV.25: And as he reasoned about righteousness and self-control and the coming judgment, Felix was alarmed and said, “Go away for the present. When I get an opportunity I will summon you.”
ESV.26: At the same time he hoped that money would be given him by Paul. So he sent for him often and conversed with him.
ESV.27: When two years had elapsed, Felix was succeeded by Porcius Festus. And desiring to do the Jews a favor, Felix left Paul in prison.

NASB.1: After five days the high priest Ananias came down with some elders, with an attorney [named] Tertullus, and they brought charges to the governor against Paul.
NASB.2: After [Paul] had been summoned, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying [to the governor], 'Since we have through you attained much peace, and since by your providence reforms are being carried out for this nation,
NASB.3: we acknowledge [this] in every way and everywhere, most excellent Felix, with all thankfulness.
NASB.4: 'But, that I may not weary you any further, I beg you to grant us, by your kindness, a brief hearing.
NASB.5: 'For we have found this man a real pest and a fellow who stirs up dissension among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes.
NASB.6: 'And he even tried to desecrate the temple; and then we arrested him. [We wanted to judge him according to our own Law.
NASB.7: 'But Lysias the commander came along, and with much violence took him out of our hands,
NASB.8: ordering his accusers to come before you]. By examining him yourself concerning all these matters you will be able to ascertain the things of which we accuse him.'
NASB.9: The Jews also joined in the attack, asserting that these things were so.
NASB.10: When the governor had nodded for him to speak, Paul responded: 'Knowing that for many years you have been a judge to this nation, I cheerfully make my defense,
NASB.11: since you can take note of the fact that no more than twelve days ago I went up to Jerusalem to worship.
NASB.12: 'Neither in the temple, nor in the synagogues, nor in the city [itself] did they find me carrying on a discussion with anyone or causing a riot.
NASB.13: 'Nor can they prove to you [the charges] of which they now accuse me.
NASB.14: 'But this I admit to you, that according to the Way which they call a sect I do serve the God of our fathers, believing everything that is in accordance with the Law and that is written in the Prophets;
NASB.15: having a hope in God, which these men cherish themselves, that there shall certainly be a resurrection of both the righteous and the wicked.
NASB.16: 'In view of this, I also do my best to maintain always a blameless conscience [both] before God and before men.
NASB.17: 'Now after several years I came to bring alms to my nation and to present offerings;
NASB.18: in which they found me [occupied] in the temple, having been purified, without [any] crowd or uproar. But [there were] some Jews from Asia--
NASB.19: who ought to have been present before you and to make accusation, if they should have anything against me.
NASB.20: 'Or else let these men themselves tell what misdeed they found when I stood before the Council,
NASB.21: other than for this one statement which I shouted out while standing among them, 'For the resurrection of the dead I am on trial before you today.''
NASB.22: But Felix, having a more exact knowledge about the Way, put them off, saying, 'When Lysias the commander comes down, I will decide your case.'
NASB.23: Then he gave orders to the centurion for him to be kept in custody and [yet] have [some] freedom, and not to prevent any of his friends from ministering to him.
NASB.24: But some days later Felix arrived with Drusilla, his wife who was a Jewess, and sent for Paul and heard him [speak] about faith in Christ Jesus.
NASB.25: But as he was discussing righteousness, self-control and the judgment to come, Felix became frightened and said, 'Go away for the present, and when I find time I will summon you.'
NASB.26: At the same time too, he was hoping that money would be given him by Paul; therefore he also used to send for him quite often and converse with him.
NASB.27: But after two years had passed, Felix was succeeded by Porcius Festus, and wishing to do the Jews a favor, Felix left Paul imprisoned.

NLT.1: Five days later Ananias, the high priest, arrived with some of the Jewish elders and the lawyer Tertullus, to present their case against Paul to the governor.
NLT.2: When Paul was called in, Tertullus presented the charges against Paul in the following address to the governor: “You have provided a long period of peace for us Jews and with foresight have enacted reforms for us.
NLT.3: For all of this, Your Excellency, we are very grateful to you.
NLT.4: But I don’t want to bore you, so please give me your attention for only a moment.
NLT.5: We have found this man to be a troublemaker who is constantly stirring up riots among the Jews all over the world. He is a ringleader of the cult known as the Nazarenes.
NLT.6: Furthermore, he was trying to desecrate the Temple when we arrested him.
NLT.8: You can find out the truth of our accusations by examining him yourself.”
NLT.9: Then the other Jews chimed in, declaring that everything Tertullus said was true.
NLT.10: The governor then motioned for Paul to speak. Paul said, “I know, sir, that you have been a judge of Jewish affairs for many years, so I gladly present my defense before you.
NLT.11: You can quickly discover that I arrived in Jerusalem no more than twelve days ago to worship at the Temple.
NLT.12: My accusers never found me arguing with anyone in the Temple, nor stirring up a riot in any synagogue or on the streets of the city.
NLT.13: These men cannot prove the things they accuse me of doing.
NLT.14: “But I admit that I follow the Way, which they call a cult. I worship the God of our ancestors, and I firmly believe the Jewish law and everything written in the prophets.
NLT.15: I have the same hope in God that these men have, that he will raise both the righteous and the unrighteous.
NLT.16: Because of this, I always try to maintain a clear conscience before God and all people.
NLT.17: “After several years away, I returned to Jerusalem with money to aid my people and to offer sacrifices to God.
NLT.18: My accusers saw me in the Temple as I was completing a purification ceremony. There was no crowd around me and no rioting.
NLT.19: But some Jews from the province of Asia were there — and they ought to be here to bring charges if they have anything against me!
NLT.20: Ask these men here what crime the Jewish high council found me guilty of,
NLT.21: except for the one time I shouted out, ‘I am on trial before you today because I believe in the resurrection of the dead!’”
NLT.22: At that point Felix, who was quite familiar with the Way, adjourned the hearing and said, “Wait until Lysias, the garrison commander, arrives. Then I will decide the case.”
NLT.23: He ordered an officer to keep Paul in custody but to give him some freedom and allow his friends to visit him and take care of his needs.
NLT.24: A few days later Felix came back with his wife, Drusilla, who was Jewish. Sending for Paul, they listened as he told them about faith in Christ Jesus.
NLT.25: As he reasoned with them about righteousness and self-control and the coming day of judgment, Felix became frightened. “Go away for now,” he replied. “When it is more convenient, I’ll call for you again.”
NLT.26: He also hoped that Paul would bribe him, so he sent for him quite often and talked with him.
NLT.27: After two years went by in this way, Felix was succeeded by Porcius Festus. And because Felix wanted to gain favor with the Jewish people, he left Paul in prison.

WEB.1: After five days, the high priest, Ananias, came down with certain elders and an orator, one Tertullus. They informed the governor against Paul.
WEB.2: When he was called, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying, “Seeing that by you we enjoy much peace, and that prosperity is coming to this nation by your foresight,
WEB.3: we accept it in all ways and in all places, most excellent Felix, with all thankfulness.
WEB.4: But, that I don’t delay you, I entreat you to bear with us and hear a few words.
WEB.5: For we have found this man to be a plague, an instigator of insurrections among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes.
WEB.6: He even tried to profane the temple, and we arrested him.
WEB.8: By examining him yourself you may ascertain all these things of which we accuse him.”
WEB.9: The Jews also joined in the attack, affirming that these things were so.
WEB.10: When the governor had beckoned to him to speak, Paul answered, “Because I know that you have been a judge of this nation for many years, I cheerfully make my defense,
WEB.11: seeing that you can recognize that it is not more than twelve days since I went up to worship at Jerusalem.
WEB.12: In the temple they didn’t find me disputing with anyone or stirring up a crowd, either in the synagogues, or in the city.
WEB.13: Nor can they prove to you the things of which they now accuse me.
WEB.14: But this I confess to you, that after the Way, which they call a sect, so I serve the God of our fathers, believing all things which are according to the law, and which are written in the prophets;
WEB.15: having hope toward God, which these also themselves look for, that there will be a resurrection of the dead, both of the just and unjust.
WEB.16: Herein I also practice always having a conscience void of offense toward God and men.
WEB.17: Now after some years, I came to bring gifts for the needy to my nation, and offerings;
WEB.18: amid which certain Jews from Asia found me purified in the temple, not with a mob, nor with turmoil.
WEB.19: They ought to have been here before you, and to make accusation, if they had anything against me.
WEB.20: Or else let these men themselves say what injustice they found in me when I stood before the council,
WEB.21: unless it is for this one thing that I cried standing among them, ‘Concerning the resurrection of the dead I am being judged before you today!’”
WEB.22: But Felix, having more exact knowledge concerning the Way, deferred them, saying, “When Lysias, the commanding officer, comes down, I will decide your case.”
WEB.23: He ordered the centurion that Paul should be kept in custody, and should have some privileges, and not to forbid any of his friends to serve him or to visit him.
WEB.24: But after some days, Felix came with Drusilla, his wife, who was a Jewess, and sent for Paul, and heard him concerning the faith in Christ Jesus.
WEB.25: As he reasoned about righteousness, self-control, and the judgment to come, Felix was terrified, and answered, “Go your way for this time, and when it is convenient for me, I will summon you.”
WEB.26: Meanwhile, he also hoped that money would be given to him by Paul, that he might release him. Therefore also he sent for him more often, and talked with him.
WEB.27: But when two years were fulfilled, Felix was succeeded by Porcius Festus, and desiring to gain favor with the Jews, Felix left Paul in bonds.

CSB17.1: Five days later Ananias <sup>ⓗ</sup> the high priest came down with some elders and a lawyer named Tertullus. These men presented their case against Paul to the governor. <sup>ⓘ</sup>
CSB17.2: When Paul was called in, Tertullus began to accuse him and said: “We enjoy great peace because of you, and reforms are taking place for the benefit of this nation because of your foresight.
CSB17.3: We acknowledge this in every way and everywhere, most excellent <sup>ⓙ</sup> Felix, with utmost gratitude.
CSB17.4: But, so that I will not burden you any further, I request that you would be kind enough to give us a brief hearing.
CSB17.5: For we have found this man to be a plague, <sup>ⓚ</sup> an agitator <sup>ⓛ</sup> among all the Jews throughout the Roman world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes. <sup>ⓜ</sup>
CSB17.6: He even tried to desecrate the temple, and so we apprehended him. <sup>[1]</sup> By examining him yourself you will be able to discern the truth about these charges we are bringing against him.”
CSB17.9: The Jews also joined in the attack, alleging that these things were true.
CSB17.10: When the governor motioned for him to speak, Paul replied: “Because I know you have been a judge of this nation for many years, I am glad to offer my defense in what concerns me. <sup>ⓝ</sup>
CSB17.11: You can verify for yourself that it is no more than twelve days since I went up to worship in Jerusalem. <sup>ⓐ</sup>
CSB17.12: They didn’t find me arguing with anyone or causing a disturbance among the crowd, either in the temple or in the synagogues or anywhere in the city. <sup>ⓑ</sup>
CSB17.13: Neither can they prove the charges they are now making against me.
CSB17.14: But I admit this to you: I worship the God of my ancestors according to the Way, <sup>ⓒ</sup> which they call a sect, believing everything that is in accordance with the law and written in the prophets. <sup>ⓓ</sup>
CSB17.15: I have a hope in God, which these men themselves also accept, that there will be a resurrection, <sup>[2]</sup> <sup>ⓔ</sup> both of the righteous and the unrighteous. <sup>ⓕ</sup>
CSB17.16: I always strive to have a clear conscience <sup>ⓖ</sup> toward God and men.
CSB17.17: After many years, I came to bring charitable gifts and offerings to my people. <sup>ⓗ</sup>
CSB17.18: While I was doing this, some Jews from Asia found me ritually purified in the temple, without a crowd and without any uproar. <sup>ⓘ</sup>
CSB17.19: It is they who ought to be here before you to bring charges, if they have anything against me.
CSB17.20: Or let these men here state what wrongdoing they found in me when I stood before the Sanhedrin,
CSB17.21: other than this one statement I shouted while standing among them, ‘Today I am on trial before you concerning the resurrection of the dead.’”
CSB17.22: Since Felix was well informed about the Way, <sup>ⓙ</sup> he adjourned the hearing, saying, “When Lysias the commander comes down, I will decide your case.”
CSB17.23: He ordered that the centurion keep Paul under guard, though he could have some freedom, and that he should not prevent any of his friends from meeting <sup>[3]</sup> his needs. <sup>ⓚ</sup>
CSB17.24: Several days later, when Felix came with his wife Drusilla, who was Jewish, he sent for Paul and listened to him on the subject of faith in Christ Jesus.
CSB17.25: Now as he spoke about righteousness, self-control, and the judgment to come, <sup>ⓛ</sup> Felix became afraid and replied, “Leave for now, but when I have an opportunity I’ll call for you.”
CSB17.26: At the same time he was also hoping that Paul would offer him money. <sup>[4]</sup> So he sent for him quite often and conversed with him.
CSB17.27: After two years had passed, Porcius Festus <sup>ⓜ</sup> succeeded Felix, and because Felix wanted to do the Jews a favor, <sup>ⓝ</sup> he left Paul in prison. <sup>ⓐ</sup>

AMP.1: Five days later, the high priest Ananias came down [from Jerusalem to Caesarea] with some elders and an attorney named Tertullus [acting as spokesman and counsel]. They presented to the governor their [formal] charges against Paul.
AMP.2:
After Paul had been summoned, Tertullus began the complaint [against him], saying to the governor:
"Since through you we have attained great peace, and since by your foresight reforms are being carried out for this nation,
AMP.3: in every way and in every place, most excellent Felix, we acknowledge this with all gratitude.
AMP.4: But so that I do not weary you further, I beg you to grant us, by your kindness, a brief hearing.
AMP.5: For we have found this man to be a public menace and one who instigates dissension among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the [heretical] sect of the Nazarenes.
AMP.6: He even tried to desecrate the temple, but we took him into custody[and we intended to judge him by our Law,
AMP.7: but Lysias the commander came, and with great force took him out of our hands,
AMP.8: and ordered his accusers to come before you.] By interrogating him yourself concerning all these matters you will be able to determine [the truth about] these things with which we charge him."
AMP.9: The Jews also joined in the attack, declaring and insisting that these things were so.
AMP.10:
When the governor nodded for him to speak, Paul answered,
"Knowing that for many years you have been a judge over this nation, I make my defense cheerfully and with good courage.
AMP.11: As you can easily verify, it has been no more than twelve days since I went up to Jerusalem to worship.
AMP.12: Neither in the temple, nor in the synagogues, nor elsewhere in the city did they find me carrying on a discussion or disputing with anybody or causing a crowd to gather.
AMP.13: Nor can they present evidence to you to prove what they now bring against me.
AMP.14: But I confess this to you, that according to the Way, which they call a [divisive and heretical] sect, I do worship and serve the God of our fathers, [confidently] believing everything that is in accordance with the Law [of Moses] and that is written in the Prophets;
AMP.15: having [the same] hope in God which these men cherish themselves, that there shall certainly be a resurrection of [the dead], both of the righteous and of the wicked.
AMP.16: In view of this, I also do my best and strive always to have a clear conscience before God and before men.
AMP.17: Now after several years I came [to Jerusalem] to bring to my people charitable contributions and offerings.
AMP.18: They found me in the temple presenting these offerings, after I had undergone [the rites of] purification, without any crowd or uproar. But there were some Jews from [the west coast province of] Asia [Minor],
AMP.19: who ought to have been here before you to present their charges, if they have anything against me.
AMP.20: Or else let these men tell what crime they found [me guilty of] when I stood before the Council (Sanhedrin, Jewish High Court),
AMP.21: other than for this one statement which I had shouted out as I stood among them, 'For the resurrection of the dead I am on trial before you today.'"
AMP.22: But Felix, having a rather accurate understanding about the Way, put them off, saying, "When Lysias the commander comes down, I will decide your case."
AMP.23: Then he ordered the centurion to keep Paul in custody, but to let him have some freedom, and [he told the centurion] not to stop any of his friends from providing for his needs.
AMP.24: Several days later Felix came with his wife Drusilla, who was a Jewess. He sent for Paul and listened to him talk about faith in Christ Jesus.
AMP.25: But as he was discussing righteousness, self-control [honorable behavior, personal integrity], and the judgment to come, Felix became frightened and said, "Go away for now, and when I find [a convenient] time I will send for you."
AMP.26: At the same time he was also hoping to get money from Paul [as a bribe]; so he continued to send for him quite often and talked with him.
AMP.27: But after two years had passed, Felix was succeeded [in office] by Porcius Festus; and wishing to do the Jews a favor, Felix left Paul imprisoned.

MSG.1: Within five days, the Chief Priest Ananias arrived with a contingent of leaders, along with Tertullus, a trial lawyer. They presented the governor with their case against Paul.
MSG.2: When Paul was called before the court, Tertullus spoke for the prosecution: "Most Honorable Felix, we are most grateful in all times and places for your wise and gentle rule.
MSG.3: We are much aware that it is because of you and you alone that we enjoy all this peace and gain daily profit from your reforms.
MSG.4: I'm not going to tire you out with a long speech. I beg your kind indulgence in listening to me. I'll be quite brief.
MSG.5: "We've found this man time and again disturbing the peace, stirring up riots against Jews all over the world, the ringleader of a seditious sect called Nazarenes.
MSG.6: He's a real bad apple, I must say. We caught him trying to defile our holy Temple and arrested him.
MSG.8: You'll be able to verify all these accusations when you examine him yourself."
MSG.9: The Jews joined in: "Hear, hear! That's right!"
MSG.10: The governor motioned to Paul that it was now his turn. Paul said, "I count myself fortunate to be defending myself before you, Governor, knowing how fair-minded you've been in judging us all these years.
MSG.11: I've been back in the country only twelve days—you can check out these dates easily enough. I came with the express purpose of worshiping in Jerusalem on Pentecost, and I've been minding my own business the whole time.
MSG.12: Nobody can say they saw me arguing in the Temple or working up a crowd in the streets.
MSG.13: Not one of their charges can be backed up with evidence or witnesses.
MSG.14: "But I do freely admit this: In regard to the Way, which they malign as a dead-end street, I serve and worship the very same God served and worshiped by all our ancestors and embrace everything written in all our Scriptures.
MSG.15: And I admit to living in hopeful anticipation that God will raise the dead, both the good and the bad. If that's my crime, my accusers are just as guilty as I am.
MSG.16: "Believe me, I do my level best to keep a clear conscience before God and my neighbors in everything I do.
MSG.17: I've been out of the country for a number of years and now I'm back. While I was away, I took up a collection for the poor and brought that with me, along with offerings for the Temple.
MSG.18: It was while making those offerings that they found me quietly at my prayers in the Temple. There was no crowd, there was no disturbance.
MSG.19: It was some Jews from around Ephesus who started all this trouble. And you'll notice they're not here today. They're cowards, too cowardly to accuse me in front of you.
MSG.20: "So ask these others what crime they've caught me in. Don't let them hide behind this smooth-talking Tertullus.
MSG.21: The only thing they have on me is that one sentence I shouted out in the council: 'It's because I believe in the resurrection that I've been hauled into this court!' Does that sound to you like grounds for a criminal case?"
MSG.22: Felix shilly-shallied. He knew far more about the Way than he let on, and could have settled the case then and there. But uncertain of his best move politically, he played for time. "When Captain Lysias comes down, I'll decide your case."
MSG.23: He gave orders to the centurion to keep Paul in custody, but to more or less give him the run of the place and not prevent his friends from helping him.
MSG.24: A few days later Felix and his wife, Drusilla, who was Jewish, sent for Paul and listened to him talk about a life of believing in Jesus Christ.
MSG.25: As Paul continued to insist on right relations with God and his people, about a life of moral discipline and the coming Judgment, Felix felt things getting a little too close for comfort and dismissed him. "That's enough for today. I'll call you back when it's convenient."
MSG.26: At the same time he was secretly hoping that Paul would offer him a substantial bribe. These conversations were repeated frequently.
MSG.27: After two years of this, Felix was replaced by Porcius Festus. Still playing up to the Jews and ignoring justice, Felix left Paul in prison.

NIV.1:
The Trial Before Felix
Five days later the high priest Ananias went down to Caesarea with some of the elders and a lawyer named Tertullus, and they brought their charges against Paul before the governor.
NIV.2: When Paul was called in, Tertullus presented his case before Felix: “We have enjoyed a long period of peace under you, and your foresight has brought about reforms in this nation.
NIV.3: Everywhere and in every way, most excellent Felix, we acknowledge this with profound gratitude.
NIV.4: But in order not to weary you further, I would request that you be kind enough to hear us briefly.
NIV.5: “We have found this man to be a troublemaker, stirring up riots among the Jews all over the world. He is a ringleader of the Nazarene sect
NIV.6: and even tried to desecrate the temple; so we seized him.
NIV.8: By examining him yourself you will be able to learn the truth about all these charges we are bringing against him.”
NIV.9: The Jews joined in the accusation, asserting that these things were true.
NIV.10: When the governor motioned for him to speak, Paul replied: “I know that for a number of years you have been a judge over this nation; so I gladly make my defense.
NIV.11: You can easily verify that no more than twelve days ago I went up to Jerusalem to worship.
NIV.12: My accusers did not find me arguing with anyone at the temple, or stirring up a crowd in the synagogues or anywhere else in the city.
NIV.13: And they cannot prove to you the charges they are now making against me.
NIV.14: However, I admit that I worship the God of our fathers as a follower of the Way, which they call a sect. I believe everything that agrees with the Law and that is written in the Prophets,
NIV.15: and I have the same hope in God as these men, that there will be a resurrection of both the righteous and the wicked.
NIV.16: So I strive always to keep my conscience clear before God and man.
NIV.17: “After an absence of several years, I came to Jerusalem to bring my people gifts for the poor and to present offerings.
NIV.18: I was ceremonially clean when they found me in the temple courts doing this. There was no crowd with me, nor was I involved in any disturbance.
NIV.19: But there are some Jews from the province of Asia, who ought to be here before you and bring charges if they have anything against me.
NIV.20: Or these who are here should state what crime they found in me when I stood before the Sanhedrin—
NIV.21: unless it was this one thing I shouted as I stood in their presence: ‘It is concerning the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial before you today.’”
NIV.22: Then Felix, who was well acquainted with the Way, adjourned the proceedings. “When Lysias the commander comes,” he said, “I will decide your case.”
NIV.23: He ordered the centurion to keep Paul under guard but to give him some freedom and permit his friends to take care of his needs.
NIV.24: Several days later Felix came with his wife Drusilla, who was a Jewess. He sent for Paul and listened to him as he spoke about faith in Christ Jesus.
NIV.25: As Paul discoursed on righteousness, self-control and the judgment to come, Felix was afraid and said, “That’s enough for now! You may leave. When I find it convenient, I will send for you.”
NIV.26: At the same time he was hoping that Paul would offer him a bribe, so he sent for him frequently and talked with him.
NIV.27: When two years had passed, Felix was succeeded by Porcius Festus, but because Felix wanted to grant a favor to the Jews, he left Paul in prison.

NRSVCE.1: Five days later the high priest Ananias came down with some elders and an attorney, a certain Tertullus, and they reported their case against Paul to the governor.
NRSVCE.2: When Paul had been summoned, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying: “Your Excellency, because of you we have long enjoyed peace, and reforms have been made for this people because of your foresight.
NRSVCE.3: We welcome this in every way and everywhere with utmost gratitude.
NRSVCE.4: But, to detain you no further, I beg you to hear us briefly with your customary graciousness.
NRSVCE.5: We have, in fact, found this man a pestilent fellow, an agitator among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes.
NRSVCE.6: He even tried to profane the temple, and so we seized him.
NRSVCE.8: By examining him yourself you will be able to learn from him concerning everything of which we accuse him.”
NRSVCE.9: The Jews also joined in the charge by asserting that all this was true.
NRSVCE.10: When the governor motioned to him to speak, Paul replied: “I cheerfully make my defense, knowing that for many years you have been a judge over this nation.
NRSVCE.11: As you can find out, it is not more than twelve days since I went up to worship in Jerusalem.
NRSVCE.12: They did not find me disputing with anyone in the temple or stirring up a crowd either in the synagogues or throughout the city.
NRSVCE.13: Neither can they prove to you the charge that they now bring against me.
NRSVCE.14: But this I admit to you, that according to the Way, which they call a sect, I worship the God of our ancestors, believing everything laid down according to the law or written in the prophets.
NRSVCE.15: I have a hope in God — a hope that they themselves also accept — that there will be a resurrection of both the righteous and the unrighteous.
NRSVCE.16: Therefore I do my best always to have a clear conscience toward God and all people.
NRSVCE.17: Now after some years I came to bring alms to my nation and to offer sacrifices.
NRSVCE.18: While I was doing this, they found me in the temple, completing the rite of purification, without any crowd or disturbance.
NRSVCE.19: But there were some Jews from Asia — they ought to be here before you to make an accusation, if they have anything against me.
NRSVCE.20: Or let these men here tell what crime they had found when I stood before the council,
NRSVCE.21: unless it was this one sentence that I called out while standing before them, ‘It is about the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial before you today.’”
NRSVCE.22: But Felix, who was rather well informed about the Way, adjourned the hearing with the comment, “When Lysias the tribune comes down, I will decide your case.”
NRSVCE.23: Then he ordered the centurion to keep him in custody, but to let him have some liberty and not to prevent any of his friends from taking care of his needs.
NRSVCE.24: Some days later when Felix came with his wife Drusilla, who was Jewish, he sent for Paul and heard him speak concerning faith in Christ Jesus.
NRSVCE.25: And as he discussed justice, self-control, and the coming judgment, Felix became frightened and said, “Go away for the present; when I have an opportunity, I will send for you.”
NRSVCE.26: At the same time he hoped that money would be given him by Paul, and for that reason he used to send for him very often and converse with him.
NRSVCE.27: After two years had passed, Felix was succeeded by Porcius Festus; and since he wanted to grant the Jews a favor, Felix left Paul in prison.

NET.1: After five days the high priest Ananias came down with some elders and an attorney named Tertullus, and they brought formal charges against Paul to the governor.
NET.2: When Paul had been summoned, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying, “We have experienced a lengthy time of peace through your rule, and reforms are being made in this nation through your foresight.
NET.3: Most excellent Felix, we acknowledge this everywhere and in every way with all gratitude.
NET.4: But so that I may not delay you any further, I beg you to hear us briefly with your customary graciousness.
NET.5: For we have found this man to be a troublemaker, one who stirs up riots among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes.
NET.6: He even tried to desecrate the temple, so we arrested him.
NET.8: When you examine him yourself, you will be able to learn from him about all these things we are accusing him of doing.”
NET.9: The Jews also joined in the verbal attack, claiming that these things were true.
NET.10: When the governor gestured for him to speak, Paul replied, “Because I know that you have been a judge over this nation for many years, I confidently make my defense.
NET.11: As you can verify for yourself, not more than twelve days ago I went up to Jerusalem to worship.
NET.12: They did not find me arguing with anyone or stirring up a crowd in the temple courts or in the synagogues or throughout the city,
NET.13: nor can they prove to you the things they are accusing me of doing.
NET.14: But I confess this to you, that I worship the God of our ancestors according to the Way (which they call a sect), believing everything that is according to the law and that is written in the prophets.
NET.15: I have a hope in God (a hope that these men themselves accept too) that there is going to be a resurrection of both the righteous and the unrighteous.
NET.16: This is the reason I do my best to always have a clear conscience toward God and toward people.
NET.17: After several years I came to bring to my people gifts for the poor and to present offerings,
NET.18: which I was doing when they found me in the temple, ritually purified, without a crowd or a disturbance.
NET.19: But there are some Jews from the province of Asia who should be here before you and bring charges, if they have anything against me.
NET.20: Or these men here should tell what crime they found me guilty of when I stood before the council,
NET.21: other than this one thing I shouted out while I stood before them: ‘I am on trial before you today concerning the resurrection of the dead.’”
NET.22: Then Felix, who understood the facts concerning the Way more accurately, adjourned their hearing, saying, “When Lysias the commanding officer comes down, I will decide your case.”
NET.23: He ordered the centurion to guard Paul, but to let him have some freedom, and not to prevent any of his friends from meeting his needs.
NET.24: Some days later, when Felix arrived with his wife Drusilla, who was Jewish, he sent for Paul and heard him speak about faith in Christ Jesus.
NET.25: While Paul was discussing righteousness, self-control, and the coming judgment, Felix became frightened and said, “Go away for now, and when I have an opportunity, I will send for you.”
NET.26: At the same time he was also hoping that Paul would give him money, and for this reason he sent for Paul as often as possible and talked with him.
NET.27: After two years had passed, Porcius Festus succeeded Felix, and because he wanted to do the Jews a favor, Felix left Paul in prison.
